variables.content: One-shot G_{0}W_{0} quasiparticle calculations that fully include relativistic spin–orbit coupling, performed for two structural models of CsPbBr3, yield the following valence-band hole effective masses and quasiparticle band gaps: for the cubic perovskite structure, an effective hole mass at the R point evaluated along the X–R direction of m_{h}^{*}=0.15\,m_{0} with a quasiparticle band gap E_{\mathrm{g}}^{\mathrm{QP}}=1.74\ \mathrm{eV}; for an orthorhombic structural unit cell, a G_{0}W_{0} calculation (with the valence band Wannier-interpolated for the purpose of band-curvature averaging) yields an averaged hole effective mass m_{h}^{*}=0.226\,m_{0} with quasiparticle band gap E_{\mathrm{g}}^{\mathrm{QP}}=2.09\ \mathrm{eV}. These one-shot G_{0}W_{0} quasiparticle results include electron–electron correlation effects via the GW self-energy but do not include electron–phonon self-energy contributions (i.e., they are bare-band quasiparticle properties with respect to phonons).

With spin–orbit coupling (SOC) included as described in the parametrization, the DFTB calculation for the orthorhombic γ-CsPbI₃ phase yields a band gap E_g = 1.70 eV, an average electron effective mass m_e = 0.22 m_0, an average hole effective mass m_h = 0.27 m_0, and a reduced effective mass μ = m_e m_h/(m_e + m_h) = 0.12 m_0 (where m_0 denotes the free-electron rest mass); this reduced mass value is in close agreement with the experimental reduced mass μ_exp = 0.114 m_0, and the DFTB-computed band-structure shape, band-edge locations and average effective masses are consistent with reference self-consistent-scissor GW (ss-GW) quasiparticle calculations used for validation.

Single-shot many-body quasiparticle calculations in the G0W0 approximation performed on top of a PBE0 hybrid Kohn–Sham starting point with spin–orbit coupling included (denoted G0W0@PBE0) predict a 0 K fundamental indirect band gap of 1.77 eV for orthorhombic BiSI and a direct transition only ≈20 meV higher in energy; the calculated electron effective mass is m_e* = 0.53 m_0 and the hole effective mass is m_h* = 0.95 m_0 (where m_0 is the free-electron mass), indicating more dispersive conduction bands consistent with strong spin–orbit coupling from Bi, and the ionization potential computed from electrostatic slab calculations and corrected by the quasiparticle shift is 6.33 eV below the vacuum level.

For orthorhombic CsPbBr3 measured at 2 K, the band-gap energy determined from the 1s excitonic absorption is E_g = 2342 meV, the exciton binding energy determined from the hydrogenic exciton fits is R* = 33 ± 1 meV, the exciton reduced mass determined from the high-field inter–Landau-level fits is μ = 0.126 ± 0.01 m_0, and the effective dielectric constant inferred from R* via R* = R_0 (μ/m_0) / ε_eff^2 is ε_eff = 7.3.

Single‑shot G0W0 quasiparticle calculations carried out on top of HSE06 Kohn–Sham eigenvalues and wavefunctions tend to produce quasiparticle band gaps larger than the HSE06+SOC Kohn–Sham gaps when the screened Coulomb interaction W is constructed using the random‑phase approximation (RPA) dielectric function ε_RPA (i.e., W = ε_RPA^(−1)v with v the bare Coulomb kernel); including electron–hole attraction (the test‑charge–test‑charge, TC–TC, or excitonic contribution in the screening used to form W) reduces the effective electron–electron interaction and substantially reduces the G0W0 overestimation, bringing the G0W0^{TC-TC} gaps into closer agreement with experiment for InP and InAs; for InSb a remaining discrepancy in G0W0^{TC-TC} quasiparticle energies is attributed to the common omission of SOC when computing the dielectric screening, because when the valence‑band split‑off energy ΔSO becomes comparable to the fundamental gap the SOC‑induced redistribution of valence‑band oscillator strength and the shift of the valence‑band center‑of‑mass significantly modify the static and low‑frequency dielectric response ε( q, ω→0 ) and therefore W.

Single-shot $G_{0}W_{0}$ quasiparticle calculations performed on top of Kohn–Sham orbitals obtained from the HSE06 hybrid density functional including spin–orbit coupling (SOC) and with a tuned fraction $\alpha$ of exact Hartree–Fock exchange produce direct bandgaps (conduction-band minimum and valence-band maximum located at the $R$-point of the cubic Brillouin zone, $R=(0.5,0.5,0.5)$) of $E_{g}=2.37\ \mathrm{eV}$ for MAPbBr$_{3}$ with $\alpha=50\%$, $E_{g}=2.27\ \mathrm{eV}$ for FAPbBr$_{3}$ with $\alpha=40\%$, and $E_{g}=2.30\ \mathrm{eV}$ for CsPbBr$_{3}$ with $\alpha=55\%$; parabolic fits to the $G_{0}W_{0}$ quasiparticle band dispersions including SOC in small $k$-space regions around the band extrema yield electron and hole effective masses whose harmonic combination $\mu^{-1}=m_{e}^{*-1}+m_{h}^{*-1}$ gives reduced masses $\mu$ (in units of the free-electron mass $m_{0}$) of $\mu=0.113$ for MAPbBr$_{3}$, $\mu=0.115$ for FAPbBr$_{3}$, and $\mu=0.080$ for CsPbBr$_{3}$.

Using ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) sampling of finite-temperature atomic configurations for bulk CsPbBr₃ at temperature T = 300 K in the canonical (NVT) ensemble with a Nosé–Hoover thermostat on a 3×3×3 pseudocubic supercell (135 atoms) and a 1 fs time step, and computing single-point electronic structures on a representative equilibrated AIMD snapshot with the Heyd–Scuseria–Ernzerhof hybrid functional including spin–orbit coupling (HSE–SOC, where HSE denotes the screened hybrid functional of Heyd, Scuseria, and Ernzerhof and SOC denotes spin–orbit coupling) using a hybrid mixing parameter α = 0.54 chosen so that the calculated band gap E_g = 2.37 eV matches the experimental room-temperature band gap, the direction-averaged hole effective mass computed for that room-temperature AIMD configuration is m_h = 0.265 m_0 (where m_0 is the free-electron mass). This computed m_h = 0.265 m_0 agrees with the angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) measurement of m_h = 0.26 ± 0.02 m_0 reported for CsPbBr₃, and this agreement is obtained without introducing any extra hole into the supercell (i.e., without polaron formation); therefore the ARPES-measured hole effective mass can be explained by thermal-lattice fluctuations alone under these simulated room-temperature, bulk-like conditions.

Carrier effective masses extracted from the curvature of the computed single‑particle bands yield the following ranges (m0 denotes the free‑electron mass): for the cubic phase, electron effective mass m_e* without SOC = 0.144–0.215 m0 and with SOC = 0.068–0.082 m0, and hole effective mass m_h* without SOC = 0.069–0.095 m0 and with SOC = 0.063–0.084 m0; for the tetragonal phase, m_e* without SOC = 0.130–0.178 m0 and with SOC = 0.056–0.073 m0, and m_h* without SOC = 0.077–0.082 m0 and with SOC = 0.056–0.064 m0; for the orthorhombic phase, m_e* without SOC = 0.165–0.254 m0 and with SOC = 0.067–0.087 m0, and m_h* without SOC = 0.064–0.113 m0 and with SOC = 0.069–0.081 m0. These DFT‑derived effective masses were used to compute electron–hole reduced masses μ = (m_e* m_h*)/(m_e* + m_h*) for subsequent exciton estimates.

Carrier effective masses extracted from parabolic fits to the band extrema using m_e* = ℏ^2 [∂^2 ε_c(k)/∂k^2]^{-1} for electrons and m_h* = ℏ^2 [∂^2 ε_v(k)/∂k^2]^{-1} for holes (with ε_c(k) = ε_0c + ℏ^2 k^2/(2m_e*) and ε_v(k) = ε_0v − ℏ^2 k^2/(2m_h*)) are small for all examined ClNH3PbI3 polymorphs and are strongly reduced when spin–orbit coupling is included. Without SOC the mean effective masses (in units of the free electron mass m_0) are (m_h*, m_e*) = (0.84, 1.14) m_0 for pseudocubic [110], (0.23, 0.38) m_0 for pseudocubic [111], and (0.32, 0.35) m_0 for the orthorhombic polymorph; with SOC the corresponding values become (0.68, 0.25) m_0, (0.17, 0.12) m_0, and (0.28, 0.22) m_0, respectively. Thus inclusion of SOC reduces both hole and electron effective masses, reduces the electron mass more strongly than the hole mass, and in these cases reverses the non-SOC ordering so that the electron effective mass becomes smaller than the hole effective mass after SOC is included.

The Kohn–Sham fundamental band gap E_{g} (energy difference between the conduction-band minimum and valence-band maximum computed from the PBE-GGA plane-wave calculations described above) for cubic CsPbBr3 has its valence-band maximum and conduction-band minimum located at the R high-symmetry point of the cubic Brillouin zone (i.e., the gap is direct at R), and the computed E_{g} values as a function of lattice parameter a (in ångströms) are: a = 5.6 ⇒ E_{g} = 0.8 eV; a = 5.7 ⇒ E_{g} = 1.2 eV; a = 5.8 ⇒ E_{g} = 1.29 eV; a = 5.9 ⇒ E_{g} = 1.49 eV; a = 6.0 ⇒ E_{g} = 1.71 eV; a = 6.1 ⇒ E_{g} = 1.90 eV; a = 6.2 ⇒ E_{g} = 1.98 eV; a = 6.3 ⇒ E_{g} = 2.10 eV; across this sampled range E_{g} increases with increasing a, increasing rapidly for a < 6.0 Å and exhibiting a plateauing (slower increase) for a > 6.0 Å in the reported dataset.
